params.text:
Understanding malnutrition

Malnutrition means the body is not getting enough energy and nutrients to stay healthy. It is common in older and unwell people, and it often develops slowly and unnoticed. Spotting it early gives the best chance of putting things right.

  • What it is: too little energy, protein or nutrients over time.
  • Causes: poor appetite, illness, low mood, dementia or swallowing problems.
  • Signs: weight loss, loose clothes or dentures, tiredness, poor healing.

Screening and monitoring

To pick up malnutrition, many services use a screening tool called MUST (the Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool, from BAPEN). It helps identify adults who are malnourished or at risk, so they can get the right support.

You may be asked to weigh people regularly, watch for unplanned weight loss and record what someone eats on a food chart. Report any concerns — such as clothes becoming loose or meals left unfinished — to a nurse or dietitian so they can act.

params.question: What is the MUST tool used for?

answers.text: Screening adults to identify those who are malnourished or at risk of malnutrition.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. MUST flags who needs further assessment and support around nutrition.
MUST is about nutrition risk, not blood pressure.

answers.text: Recording how much fluid a person drinks.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: That is a fluid balance chart. MUST screens for malnutrition risk.

answers.text: A safe method for moving and handling people.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: MUST is a malnutrition screening tool, not a moving and handling technique.
